overview: Processes files stored inside ZIP archive files (e.g. `.zip` or `.jar` files). It extracts files matching specified name patterns from the archive and processes each one using a designated Okapi filter configuration. This enables localization of translatable files embedded within compressed archives.

identification.mimeType.help: The MIME type for the **archive container** itself, not for the files inside it. Typically left at the default unless you are working with a custom archive format.

identification.fileNames.help:
Comma-separated list of file names or patterns to extract from the archive. Files matching these patterns (in any directory inside the archive) will be processed.

- Supports wildcards: `?` (single character) and `*` (any characters)
- Each entry corresponds positionally to an entry in **Filter configuration IDs**
- The number of file name entries must match the number of filter configuration IDs
identification.fileNames.values: Comma-delimited file name patterns. Wildcards `?` and `*` are supported.
examples: `Res.properties` — matches files named exactly `Res.properties` in any directory
`*.tmx,*.xlf` — matches all TMX and XLIFF files
`messages_*.properties` — matches all properties files starting with `messages_`

dependsOn.condition: must have the same number of comma-separated entries, in matching order

identification.configIds.help:
Comma-separated list of Okapi filter configuration IDs, one for each file name pattern. Each ID tells the bridge which filter to use when processing the matched files.

- Must have exactly the same number of entries as **File names**
- Entries are positionally matched: the first config ID applies to files matching the first file name pattern, and so on
examples: `okf_properties` — process matched files with the Properties filter (default config)
`okf_tmx,okf_xliff,okf_xliff` — three filters matching three file name patterns

dependsOn.condition: must have the same number of comma-separated entries, in matching order

processingNotes: Files are matched by name pattern **regardless of their directory path** inside the archive — a pattern like `Res.properties` matches that filename in any subdirectory.
Each file name pattern is paired positionally with a filter configuration ID — the first pattern uses the first config ID, the second pattern uses the second config ID, etc.

examples.title: Extract properties files from a JAR
examples.description: Process all `.properties` files inside a JAR archive using the Properties filter.

examples.title: Multiple file types in one archive
examples.description: Extract and process both TMX and XLIFF files from a ZIP archive, each with the appropriate filter.
